event: Early hominins inhabit the region, with stone tools later found at sites such as Kalambo Falls testifying to deep human presence.
The skull known as Broken Hill Man (Kabwe 1, Homo heidelbergensis) lived near present-day Kabwe, one of Africa's most important early human fossils.
Late Stone Age hunter-gatherer communities, ancestors of the San (Khoisan) peoples, occupy the region, leaving rock art across the area.
Bantu-speaking farmers begin migrating into the region, introducing iron-working, agriculture, and pottery.
Iron Age Bantu communities establish settled farming villages along rivers and lakes across the territory.
Trading networks linking the interior to the Indian Ocean coast develop, exchanging copper, ivory, and gold.
Copper mining and the use of copper cross-shaped ingots (handa) as currency flourish in the Copperbelt region.
Successive waves of Bantu migration bring groups such as the Tonga, Tumbuka, and others who form distinct chiefdoms.
The Maravi Confederacy expands its influence into eastern parts of the region.
Lunda and Luba peoples migrate from the Congo region, founding kingdoms including those of the Lunda and the Kazembe.
The Eastern Lunda Kingdom of Kazembe is established in the Luapula Valley, becoming a major power and trade hub.
Portuguese explorer Francisco de Lacerda reaches the court of the Kazembe, among the first documented European contacts.
The Mfecane upheavals push Nguni and Kololo groups northward; the Kololo later conquer the Lozi (Barotse) kingdom.
Sebitwane leads the Kololo people across the Zambezi, establishing dominance over the Barotse floodplain.
Scottish missionary and explorer David Livingstone becomes the first European to view and name Victoria Falls (Mosi-oa-Tunya).
The Lozi under Sipopa Lutangu overthrow Kololo rule and restore the Barotse (Lozi) kingdom.
Lozi king Lewanika signs concessions with agents of Cecil Rhodes, opening the territory to the British South Africa Company.
The British South Africa Company secures mineral and treaty rights, beginning company rule over the region.
Barotseland-North-Western Rhodesia is established as a British protectorate administered by the company.
North-Western and North-Eastern Rhodesia are amalgamated into the single territory of Northern Rhodesia.
Administration of Northern Rhodesia is transferred from the British South Africa Company to the British Crown as a protectorate.
Vast copper deposits are confirmed in the Copperbelt, triggering large-scale mining development and labour migration.
African mineworkers stage major strikes on the Copperbelt protesting low wages and discriminatory conditions.
The Northern Rhodesia African Congress, an early African nationalist movement, is founded.
Northern Rhodesia is incorporated into the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland despite African opposition.
Kenneth Kaunda breaks away to form the Zambia African National Congress, intensifying the independence struggle.
Kaunda's party is banned and he is imprisoned, but the nationalist movement gains momentum.
Kaunda becomes leader of the United National Independence Party (UNIP), spearheading the drive for independence.
The Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland is dissolved, paving the way for Northern Rhodesia's self-rule.
Zambia gains independence on 24 October, with Kenneth Kaunda as its first president, and joins the United Nations.
Zambia faces economic pressure after neighbouring Rhodesia's white-minority government declares unilateral independence.
Zambia hosts the Non-Aligned Movement summit in Lusaka, affirming its role in anti-colonial diplomacy.
Kaunda declares Zambia a one-party state with UNIP as the sole legal party.
The TAZARA railway, built with Chinese assistance, is largely completed, linking Zambia to Tanzania's port at Dar es Salaam.
A sharp fall in world copper prices plunges Zambia's economy into prolonged crisis and rising debt.
An attempted coup against President Kaunda is foiled.
Austerity measures and removal of food subsidies under IMF pressure spark deadly food riots on the Copperbelt.
Mounting protests force Kaunda to agree to restore multiparty democracy.
In Zambia's first multiparty elections in decades, Frederick Chiluba defeats Kaunda to become president.
Chiluba is re-elected after constitutional changes controversially bar Kaunda from contesting.
Levy Mwanawasa wins the presidency and later launches a high-profile anti-corruption campaign.
Zambia qualifies for major debt relief under the HIPC initiative, easing its long-standing debt burden.
President Levy Mwanawasa dies in office and is succeeded by Rupiah Banda.
Opposition leader Michael Sata wins the presidency, marking a peaceful transfer of power.
President Michael Sata dies in office; Edgar Lungu later wins the resulting election.
Zambia becomes the first African nation to default on its sovereign debt during the COVID-19 pandemic era.
Opposition leader Hakainde Hichilema decisively defeats incumbent Edgar Lungu to become president.
Zambia reaches a landmark restructuring agreement with creditors as a severe drought devastates agriculture and hydropower.
